name: Sevier Canyon
short_description: BLM public land in central Utah's high desert where colorful agate, chalcedony, and jasper specimens can be found in canyon washes and hillside deposits. Personal use rockhounding with no permit re...
description: Sevier Canyon offers rockhounders access to quality agate and chalcedony specimens across BLM-managed public land in Sevier County. The canyon cuts through mineralized terrain typical of Utah's high desert plateau, where ancient hydrothermal activity deposited silica-rich minerals throughout the surrounding rock formations. Collectors can explore exposed canyon walls, wash gravels, and nearby hillsides for banded agates, colorful chalcedony nodules, and jasper pieces ranging from thumbnail to fist-sized specimens. The area is particularly rewarding after seasonal rains when erosion exposes fresh material in the canyon bottoms. Being on BLM land means no permits are required for recreational collecting, making this an accessible destination for both beginners and experienced rockhounders seeking quality desert material without the fees of commercial dig sites. The remote high-desert setting provides solitude and good visibility for spotting specimens across the open terrain.
history: This region sits within the Colorado Plateau province where Oligocene to Miocene volcanic and hydrothermal activity created the silica-rich mineral deposits found throughout Sevier County. Ancient hot springs and mineral-bearing fluids percolating through limestone and volcanic rock formations deposited the agates, chalcedony, and jasper specimens that erode out naturally today, making this canyon an excellent natural collection site.
tips: Focus your search in the main canyon wash and along exposed streambed gravels where material has been naturally concentrated and sorted by water flow. Look for banded agate with warm earth tones (reds, browns, tans) and translucent chalcedony nodules - these often have a waxy luster when freshly exposed. Bring a small pick or rock hammer to break away specimens from loose hillside outcrops, and inspect the area after recent rains when erosion has freshly exposed new material. Work early morning to avoid afternoon sun heat and check carefully around the base of rock ledges where specimens tend to accumulate.
terrain_notes: The canyon sits at approximately 5,500 feet elevation with open high-desert terrain featuring sparse sagebrush and desert vegetation. The main canyon wash is relatively accessible with sandy and gravelly footing that can be uneven in places. Hillsides adjacent to the canyon are moderate slopes with exposed rocky outcrops and loose scree that require careful footing. Walking distance from parking area to productive collecting grounds is typically under 1 mile. The terrain is semi-arid with minimal shade and excellent visibility across open slopes.
best_season: April - May and September - October provide ideal conditions with moderate temperatures and lower risk of intense afternoon heat. Summer months (June - August) are extremely hot in this high-desert location and should be avoided unless collecting very early morning. Winter (November - March) can bring occasional snow and cold temperatures at this elevation, though collectors may still have success on clear days.
written_directions: From Sevier (approximately 25 miles away), take US 89 south toward the Sevier Plateau region. Turn east onto local access roads that provide access to BLM land parcels in the canyon area. GPS coordinates 38.543122, -112.389595 will direct you to the general location. Due to the remote nature and variable road conditions, verify current access routes with the Richfield BLM Field Office before your visit, as seasonal conditions and road maintenance can affect access.
road_conditions: Dirt/gravel roads with variable conditions. High-clearance vehicle recommended, especially after rain. Road may become impassable during winter or after heavy precipitation.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and parking areas along access roads. No developed parking lot. Capacity varies by specific access point - typically allows 2-6 vehicles.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 pounds per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection permitted. No motorized excavation or mechanized collecting equipment. Collect only loose or easily detached material - do not blast, drill, or use heavy equipment. Pack out all collected material and leave no trash.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation or heavy equipment
Collect only loose or easily detached material
No blasting or drilling
Pack out all material and trash
nearest_services: Sevier, approximately 25 miles west, offers gas, food, and water. Junction community also nearby with limited services.
accessibility_notes: Moderate terrain with uneven rocky footing on hillsides and canyon wash. No wheelchair access. Sturdy hiking boots strongly recommended. Slopes are navigable for most hikers but not suitable for those with significant mobility limitations. Plan for 1-2 mile walk over semi-rough terrain with elevation changes up to 300 feet.

faq.question: What gems can I find rockhounding at Sevier Canyon, Utah?
faq.answer: Sevier Canyon is known for quality agate, chalcedony, jasper, and quartz specimens. The banded agates often display warm earth tones of red, brown, and tan, while the chalcedony typically has a waxy luster and may be translucent. Specimens range from thumbnail-sized to several inches across.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Sevier Canyon?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational rockhounding at Sevier Canyon. The area is BLM public land where personal use collecting is allowed under FLPMA regulations. However, you are limited to 25 pounds of material per day and collection is for personal use only, not commercial purposes.

faq.question: When is the best time to visit Sevier Canyon for rockhounding?
faq.answer: April through May and September through October offer the most comfortable collecting conditions with moderate temperatures and lower heat risk. Summer months are extremely hot in this high-desert location. Winter is possible but less ideal due to occasional snow and cold temperatures at 5,500 feet elevation.

faq.question: Is Sevier Canyon rockhounding suitable for beginners and families?
faq.answer: Yes, Sevier Canyon is beginner-friendly with moderate difficulty terrain. Children ages 8 and up can participate in family rockhounding trips. The canyon wash and nearby slopes are accessible without technical skills, and the abundance of specimens makes it rewarding for collectors of all experience levels. Dogs are welcome on BLM land.

faq.question: What tools and supplies should I bring rockhounding in Sevier Canyon?
faq.answer: Bring a small rock hammer or pick for extracting specimens from loose outcrops, a collection bag or backpack, sturdy hiking boots for uneven terrain, plenty of water (at least 3 liters per person), sun protection, and a hat. The remote location offers no services, so come fully prepared. A hand lens helps identify quality specimens in the field.

faq.question: How far is Sevier Canyon from towns with services?
faq.answer: Sevier, the nearest town with gas, food, and water services, is approximately 25 miles west of Sevier Canyon. The location is remote with spotty cell service, so plan ahead and ensure your vehicle is in good condition before heading out. Check with the Richfield BLM Field Office about current road access before your visit.

meta_title: Sevier Canyon Rockhounding - Find Agate in Utah
